subtitle_lines: Three conditions decide whether the complexity earns its keep.

items.label: Scheduling
items.description: Places workloads across a fleet of nodes based on CPU, memory, and affinity rules.

items.label: Self-healing
items.description: Restarts failed containers, reschedules off dead nodes, gates traffic on health checks.

items.label: Service networking
items.description: Internal DNS, load balancing, and stable addressing for pods that come and go.

items.label: Rollouts
items.description: Declarative rolling updates with automatic rollback when health checks fail.

bottom_takeaway.label: Primitives
bottom_takeaway.body: Four capabilities you would otherwise build and maintain yourself.

items.label: Operational surface
items.description: API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager, kubelet, CNI, CSI, ingress. Each one fails in its own way.

items.label: Upgrade cadence
items.description: Kubernetes ships three minor versions a year, each supported for 14 months. Falling behind drops you out of the security patch window.

items.label: Abstraction debt
items.description: Every primitive has a dozen knobs you will eventually need to tune in production.

items.label: Observability cost
items.description: Cluster metrics, pod metrics, and application metrics are three separate systems you now own.

bottom_takeaway.label: Cost
bottom_takeaway.body: The tax is paid in engineer-hours, not dollars.

slides.heading: Three conditions make the cost worth paying

cards.title: Multi-service footprint
cards.body: You run 15 or more services that need to share infrastructure rather than each getting its own VM pool.

cards.title: Uneven traffic
cards.body: Load swings by 10x or more across the day, and paying for peak capacity around the clock is more expensive than autoscaling.

cards.title: High deployment velocity
cards.body: You ship to production several times a day across many services, and rolling updates with rollback are a first-class need.

bottom_takeaway.label: All three
bottom_takeaway.body: If all three hold, the control plane earns its keep.

slides.heading: Three conditions make the cost larger than the benefit

cards.title: Small service count
cards.body: You run under five services. The control plane will cost more to operate than the workloads it runs.

cards.title: Steady load
cards.body: Traffic is flat within a 2x range. Autoscaling is the main structural benefit of Kubernetes, and flat traffic leaves it on the table.

cards.title: No dedicated operator
cards.body: No single engineer is the unambiguous owner of the cluster. Shared ownership of distributed systems leaves outages that no one can debug.

bottom_takeaway.label: Any one
bottom_takeaway.body: Any one of these is enough to pick something simpler.

slides.heading: Three alternatives to reach for first

rows.label: ECS + Fargate
cells: AWS shops running under 20 services that want containers without managing nodes.
Locks you into AWS. Per-task pricing adds up at scale.

rows.label: PaaS (Fly.io, Render)
cells: Product teams that want Heroku ergonomics with container flexibility.
Opinionated platforms. Less control in exchange for faster shipping.

rows.label: Nomad
cells: Teams on the HashiCorp stack that want orchestration without the Kubernetes API surface.
Smaller ecosystem. Fewer third-party operators and integrations.

bottom_takeaway.label: Verdict
bottom_takeaway.body: Most teams land on one of these three and never need to look further.

slides.heading: Three questions before you adopt
slides.subheading: Answer honestly. The threshold is sharper than it looks.

decisions.question: Do you run 15 or more services in production?
decisions.answer: If no, a managed container platform will serve you better at a fraction of the operational cost.

decisions.question: Does one engineer unambiguously own the cluster?
decisions.answer: If no, pick a platform that hides the cluster entirely. Shared ownership of Kubernetes ends in unowned incidents.

decisions.question: Do you need features only Kubernetes offers, like custom operators or multi-tenant scheduling?
decisions.answer: If no, the ecosystem is not a reason on its own. Reach for it only when the alternatives cannot model your problem.

bottom_takeaway.label: Rule of thumb
bottom_takeaway.body: The threshold is roughly 15 services and one unambiguous cluster owner. Below either, the cost is larger than the benefit.

summary_lines: Kubernetes solves scheduling, self-healing, service networking, and rollouts.
The tax is a large operational surface that simpler platforms do not carry.
Adopt when you run 15 or more services and one engineer is the unambiguous owner of the cluster.
Below that threshold, a managed platform wins on cost and reliability.
